1.synthesis:
The daughters of Jerusalem's question — 'Where has your beloved gone, O most beautiful among women? Where has your beloved turned, that we may seek him with you?' (v. 1) — is the community's response to the woman's description of the beloved in chapter 5. Having heard the extended portrait of the beloved's beauty, they want to join the search. But the woman's response in vv. 2–3 reveals that she already knows where he is: 'My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and gather lilies. I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine; he grazes among the lilies.'

The apparent paradox is illuminating: the woman who in chapter 5 sought frantically for the beloved and found him not now knows exactly where he is. The search and seeking of chapter 5 has been resolved; the calm certainty of belonging has returned. She knows he is in the garden — the space repeatedly associated with their intimate union (cf. 4:12–16, 5:1) — grazing among the lilies. The 'grazing among lilies' is the beloved's characteristic activity in the garden of the beloved, a recurring image of his delight in her.

The mutual-belonging formula of v. 3 — 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' — is the second version of the formula from 2:16, with the order reversed: there, 'my beloved is mine, and I am his'; here, 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine.' The reversal places the woman's belonging to him first — a subtle but significant shift toward the posture of gift and surrender that will characterize the final version in 7:10, where 'his desire is for me' replaces the second clause entirely, emphasizing not her possession of him but his turning of desire toward her.
1.historical_context: The daughters of Jerusalem serve throughout the Song as the social community within which the private love story occurs: they are the chorus, the audience, the interlocutors who prompt the woman's declarations and whose questions drive forward the narrative. Their willingness to join the search ('that we may seek him with you') demonstrates the communal dimension of love: it generates community interest and participation. The garden-grazing image of v. 2 is a recurrent erotic metaphor — the beloved 'grazes' among the lilies that represent the woman's own beauty.
1.literary_structure: The brief section (three verses) serves as the transition between the extended wasf of chapter 5 (the woman's description of the beloved) and the beloved's responsive wasf of 6:4–10 (his description of the woman). The daughters' question and the woman's calm, certain answer — he is in the garden, I am his — resolves the crisis of chapter 5 and restores the equilibrium of mutual belonging before the beloved's new round of praise begins.
1.theological_application: The woman who was frantically seeking in chapter 5 now knows with certainty where the beloved is — and the certainty comes not from visual confirmation but from the relationship itself: she knows him well enough to know where he goes, what he does, where his delight is. This is the maturity of covenant love: the movement from anxious seeking (am I still loved? where has he gone?) to the settled knowledge of belonging ('I am his and he is mine; he is in his garden'). The second seeking has deepened the relationship rather than simply repeated chapter 3.
1.christology: The garden in which the beloved grazes among the lilies — where the church knows to find Christ even after the experience of his apparent absence — is the space of Word and sacrament: the church's confidence that Christ is present in his appointed place, among his own people. The eucharistic resonances are strong: he who 'gathered my garden' (5:1) is found 'gathering lilies' (6:3) in his own space of presence. The mutual-belonging formula — 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' — is the eucharistic declaration: this is my body, given for you; and we are his body, gathered by him.

4.pericope_label: He: You Are Beautiful, My Love
4.synthesis:
The beloved's second major wasf of the Song opens with a military comparison that echoes and elevates the earlier portrait: 'You are beautiful as Tirzah, my love, lovely as Jerusalem, awesome as an army with banners' (v. 4). Tirzah was the first capital of the northern kingdom — a city of renowned beauty; Jerusalem is the city of YHWH's dwelling, the city of the great King. To be compared to both cities is to be compared to the embodiment of earthly and covenantal beauty in one person. The 'awesome as an army with banners' (ʾayummāh kannidgālôt) is the Song's most militarily striking comparison: the woman's beauty is not merely attractive but formidable, capable of inspiring awe in the way an army in full formation does.

The repeated verse of v. 5 — 'Turn away your eyes from me, for they overwhelm me' — is unique in the wasf genre: the description of the beloved is interrupted by the beloved's own overwhelmed response to the bride's gaze. Love is not only the active describer but the one who is overcome by what it sees. The beloved must ask the bride to stop looking at him because her gaze is too powerful to bear — a remarkable reversal of the typical power dynamics of description, in which the gazer has power and the gazed-upon is passive.

The closing declaration of vv. 8–9 establishes the bride's absolute uniqueness: 'There are sixty queens and eighty concubines, and virgins without number. My dove, my perfect one, is the only one, the only one of her mother, pure to her who bore her. The young women saw her and called her blessed; the queens and concubines also, and they praised her.' In a cultural context in which royal courts maintained extensive harems, the beloved's declaration that the bride is 'the only one' among all of these is a radical claim of particular, exclusive regard: whatever her social status or competition, she is uniquely seen and uniquely loved.
4.historical_context: Tirzah (v. 4) served as the northern kingdom's capital before Samaria (1 Kgs 14:17; 16:23–24), and its name means 'pleasantness.' The pairing with Jerusalem — northern and southern capitals — suggests the beloved's beauty encompasses both halves of the divided kingdom. The military imagery of 'awesome as an army with banners' (vv. 4, 10) was used in the context of ANE military aesthetics: the unfurled standards and ordered ranks of a prepared army were considered a sight of terrible beauty. The sixty queens and eighty concubines of v. 8 may allude to Solomon's harem before its expansion to 700 wives and 300 concubines (1 Kgs 11:3).
4.literary_structure: The wasf is interrupted and then resumed: the beloved begins describing (vv. 4–5a), admits overwhelm (v. 5b), resumes description with the eyes-avoided (vv. 5c–7), pivots to uniqueness claim (vv. 8–9), and closes with the identity question (vv. 10–12). The interruption — 'turn away your eyes; they overwhelm me' — creates a moment of reciprocal vulnerability: the describer is himself described by the power of the beloved's gaze. This mutuality of power is characteristic of the Song's egalitarian love.
4.theological_application: The 'only one' declaration of v. 9 — against the backdrop of sixty queens and eighty concubines and countless virgins — is the covenant claim of exclusive love in a world of alternatives. YHWH declares Israel his unique possession (sĕgullāh) not because there are no other peoples but because covenant love singles out and makes uniquely precious. The theological scandal of election is that love does not operate by the logic of comparison but by the logic of particular attachment: among all the peoples, Israel is 'the only one'; among all souls, each is uniquely seen and loved by the God who knows every hair of every head (Luke 12:7).
4.christology: The 'awesome as an army with banners' image of v. 4 and the 'who is this that looks down like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awesome as an army with banners?' of v. 10 are applied in the Christian tradition to the church clothed in Christ's righteousness: the church militant, ordered and banner-bearing, is a formidable sight before which even the powers of darkness recoil. Ephesians 6's armor of God gives the church its militaristic beauty — not through human power but through the weapons of the Spirit. The 'only one' of v. 9 is applied to the church as Christ's unique beloved: 'one body, one Spirit... one Lord, one faith, one baptism' (Eph 4:4–5).

13.synthesis:
The single verse of 6:13 (numbered 7:1 in the Hebrew) is a transitional moment in which the woman is called back and then questioned: 'Return, return, O Shulammite, return, return, that we may look at you. Why should you look at the Shulammite, as at a dance before two armies?' The fourfold 'return' (šûbî) is an urgent, communal summons — the Song's most concentrated instance of calling-back the beloved. The Shulammite has been moving away or departing, and the chorus calls her to return so she may be seen.

The identity of 'the Shulammite' is the most debated question in Song scholarship. The designation šûlammît is a feminine form that may relate to: the town of Shunem in the Jezreel valley (making her 'the Shunammite'), the Hebrew word for 'peace' (šālôm, making her 'the peaceable one' or 'the perfect one'), or possibly a feminine form of 'Solomon' (šĕlōmōh), creating a pair — the Shulammite and Solomon as feminine and masculine forms of the same root. The dance 'before two armies' (maḥănāyim) may be a place name (the location where Jacob met angels, Gen 32:2) or a description of the Shulammite as the center of two encircling groups of admirers — armies of admirers drawn by her beauty.

The verse functions as the introduction to chapter 7's extended wasf: the Shulammite is called back so that she may be looked upon, and the look becomes the extended praise of 7:1–9. The question 'why would you look at the Shulammite?' may be the Shulammite's own modest deflection — 'why would you bother looking at me?' — to which the community responds with the entire wasf of chapter 7 as the answer.
13.historical_context: Shunem (modern Sulam) was a village in the Jezreel valley whose female residents appear elsewhere in the OT: the Shunammite woman of Elisha's ministry (2 Kgs 4) and Abishag the Shunammite who warmed David in his old age (1 Kgs 1:3). The Mahanayim location appears in the Jacob narrative as the place where the divine armies were seen, giving the 'dance at Mahanayim' an angelic or heavenly resonance: watching the Shulammite dance is like watching a dance before divine armies.
13.literary_structure: The verse serves as structural hinge between the end of chapter 6's wasf and the beginning of chapter 7's new wasf. The fourfold 'return' creates urgency and focus; the question ('why should you look?') creates the frame for the extended answer of 7:1–9. The Shulammite title, appearing only here, marks a moment of named identity in a book that otherwise refers to the woman through epithets (my love, my dove, my perfect one, my sister, my bride).
13.theological_application: The community's urgent calling-back of the Shulammite — 'return, return, return, return' — and their desire to look upon her is the posture of the community of faith toward the soul that has withdrawn. The church's calling-back of the departing member, the community's urgent desire to see and affirm the one who has drifted away, reflects this fourfold summons. The theological question embedded in the verse — 'why would you look at the Shulammite?' — is the self-deprecating question that many souls ask when the community of faith pursues them: why would you care about me? The answer is the wasf of chapter 7: because you are altogether beautiful, and we cannot stop looking.
13.christology: The fourfold 'return, return, O Shulammite' is read in the patristic tradition as the voice of the Trinity calling the soul back from sin and wandering. The urgent repetition — four times — suggests that no half-hearted call will suffice: the divine pursuit is total, repeated, insistent. This is the lost-sheep theology of Luke 15:4–7: the shepherd does not stop seeking until the lost sheep is found and returned. The question 'why should you look at the Shulammite?' is the soul's own voice of unworthiness, and the wasf of chapter 7 is Christ's answer — looking because love cannot stop looking at its beloved.
